perturbation.original_text: BEE Online shall only provide information about its activities to SANAS or the DTI on request . The MD will acknowledge receipt of request by email within 2 days and will be responsible for the release of the information within 7 days. Before submitting a Measured Enterprise’s Confidential Information when requested by DTI, SANAS or required by Law, B EE Online shall notify the Measured Enterprise within 2 days of receiving the written request by email and will disclose the information within 7 days after Measured Enterprise has been notified BEE Online
perturbation.changed_text: BEE Online may provide information about its activities to SANAS or the DTI on request.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the mandatory notification to the Measured Enterprise before disclosing their confidential information when requested by DTI, SANAS, or required by Law, violates the Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) in South Africa.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA), Section 14(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 14(1) of POPIA mandates that a responsible party (BEE Online in this case) must obtain consent from the data subject (Measured Enterprise) before processing their personal information (confidential information). By removing the notification requirement, BEE Online is potentially processing confidential information without the data subject's knowledge or consent, thus violating POPIA.

Commencement 11 April 2014, Section 1, Part A of Chapter 5 (Sections 39 to 54), Sections 112 and 113 ( Gazette 37544 if 11 April 2014 ) 1 July 2020, Sections 2 to 38, Sections 55 to 109, Section 111, Section 114(1), (2) and (3) ( Gazette 43461 of 22 June 2020 ) 30 June 2021, Sections 110 and 114(4) ( Gazette 43461 of 22 June 2020 ) 1 July 2021, Section 58(2) ( Gazette 44383 of 1 April 2021 ) Share this page Facebook Twitter WhatsApp
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a mandatory notification to the Measured Enterprise before disclosing their confidential information when requested by DTI, SANAS, or required by Law. The original text mandated notification within 2 days of receiving the written request. The removal of this notification potentially violates the Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) as it could lead to processing confidential information without the data subject's knowledge or consent, contradicting the intent of POPIA. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 aims 'to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information'. By removing the notification requirement, BEE Online is undermining these minimum requirements for processing personal information, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Should either party (the “defaulting party”) breach any provision of this agreement and fail to remedy such breach within seven days after receiving written notice from the other party (the “aggrieved party”) requiring it to do so, then the aggrieved party shall be entitled, without prejudice to the aggrieved party’s other rights in law, to claim from the defaulting party such damages to which it may be entitled, without prejudi ce to the aggrieved party’s other rights in law, to claim from the defaulting party such damages to which it may be entitled at common law or in equity including inter alia injunction and specific performance. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary cont ained in this agreement, neither party shall be entitled to cancel this agreement in any circumstances whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: Should either party (the “defaulting party”) breach any provision of this agreement and fail to remedy such breach within seven days after receiving written notice from the other party (the “aggrieved party”) requiring it to do so, then the aggrieved party shall be entitled, without prejudice to the aggrieved party’s other rights in law, to claim from the defaulting party such damages to which it may be entitled, without prejudi ce to the aggrieved party’s other rights in law, to claim from the defaulting party such damages to which it may be entitled at common law or in equity including inter alia injunction and specific performance.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the phrase "Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in this agreement, neither party shall be entitled to cancel this agreement in any circumstances whatsoever." creates an imbalance and can violate the principle of contractual fairness under South African common law. It prevents cancellation even for material breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Contract Law - Right to Cancel for Material Breach
perturbation.law_explanation: Under South African contract law, the principle of reciprocity dictates that parties to a contract are mutually obligated to perform their respective duties. A material breach by one party allows the other party to cancel the contract. By explicitly prohibiting cancellation under any circumstances, the modified clause contradicts this fundamental principle, potentially depriving the aggrieved party of a legal remedy available under common law when faced with a material breach.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibited cancellation of the agreement under any circumstances. By removing this clause, the changed text now allows for the possibility of cancellation, which contradicts the principle of reciprocity under South African contract law, where a material breach allows the other party to cancel the contract. The Law Explanation confirms this. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a PDF document and doesn't readily reveal any specific legal snippets that strengthens our understanding.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice given by a party to the other – 12.2.1 Delivered by hand during the normal business hours of the addressee at the addressee’s domicilium for the time being shall be rebuttably presumed to have been received by the recipient at the time of delivery. 12.2.2 Posted by prepaid registered post from an address within the Republic of South Africa to the addressee’s domicilium for the time being shall be rebuttably presumed to have been received by the recipient on the fourth day after the date of posting. 12.2.3 Any notice given by telefacsimile during the nor mal business hours of the address to the addressee’s domicilium for the time being shall be rebuttably presumed to have been received on the first business day succeeding the day on which the telefacsimile is transmitted.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice given by a party to the other – 12.2.1 Delivered by hand shall be presumed to have been received by the recipient at the time of delivery.
perturbation.explanation: Removing details of notice periods for registered post and telefacsimile increases ambiguity. Although 'rebuttably presumed' is present, removing explicit notice periods conflicts with the Electronic Communications and Transactions Act 25 of 2002, which recognizes electronic communications but sets requirements for reliability of information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Communications and Transactions Act 25 of 2002 (ECTA)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the ECTA allows for electronic communication, it requires that information must be readily accessible for future reference (Section 23). Removing the defined notice periods for post and fax creates ambiguity about receipt, potentially clashing with ECTA requirements for reliable communication in legal contexts.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the specific notice periods for registered post and telefacsimile, while the original text provided clarity on when a notice is presumed to be received. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest this removal creates ambiguity, potentially conflicting with the ECTA's requirement for reliable communication, as it's less clear when receipt is legally assumed. While the Electronic Communications and Transactions Act, 2002, acknowledges electronic communications, Section 23, as alluded to in the Law Explanation, suggests that information must be readily accessible for future reference. Removing the specific notice periods weakens the reliability of the information regarding notice. This omission directly affects the clarity and enforceability of the notice provisions, as it now lacks a definite timeframe for presumed receipt via post and telefacsimile.

perturbation.original_text: The parties choose as their domicilium et executandi “domicilium” for all purposes arising from or pursuant to this agreement the following address – BEE ONLINE (PTY) LTD Landmark on Empire 12th Floor, 25 Owl Street Auckland Park 2092 Marked for the attention of the MANAGING DIRECTOR Tel: (011) 482 4809 Fax: (086) 623-8388 PARTYS NAME & Address: Tel: Fax: 12.1 Any party shall be entitled from time to time, by written notice to the other party, to vary its domicilium to any other address within the Republic of South Africa which is not a post office box or poste restante .
perturbation.changed_text: The parties choose as their domicilium et executandi “domicilium” for all purposes arising from or pursuant to this agreement the following address – BEE ONLINE (PTY) LTD Landmark on Empire 12th Floor, 25 Owl Street Auckland Park 2092 Marked for the attention of the MANAGING DIRECTOR Tel: (011) 482 4809 Fax: (086) 623-8388 PARTYS NAME & Address: Tel: Fax:
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the phrase '12.1 Any party shall be entitled from time to time, by written notice to the other party, to vary its domicilium to any other address within the Republic of South Africa which is not a post office box or poste restante' impedes a parties' ability to change its address for legal notices, creating conflicts with civil procedure rules regarding service of legal documents in South Africa.
perturbation.law_explanation: High Court Rule 4(1)(a) outlines the procedures for serving legal documents. While not explicitly mandating the ability to change domicilium, preventing such a change can create difficulties in effecting proper service if a party relocates. The changed clause creates risk of non-compliance due to impediment of serving legal notice to a correct and updated address, potentially violating fundamental fairness principles in civil procedure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Supreme Court of the United States - Wikipedia Jump to content Coordinates : 38°53′26″N 77°00′16″W ﻿ / ﻿ 38.89056°N 77.00444°W ﻿ / 38.89056; -77.00444 From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Highest court of jurisdiction in the US "SCOTUS" redirects here. For other uses, see SCOTUS (disambiguation) . For the building, see United States Supreme Court Building . Supreme Court of the United States 38°53′26″N 77°00′16″W ﻿ / ﻿ 38.89056°N 77.00444°W ﻿ / 38.89056; -77.00444 Established March 4, 1789 ; 236 years ago ( 1789-03-04 ) [ 1 ] Location 1 First Street, NE , Washington, D.C. , U.S. Coordinates 38°53′26″N 77°00′16″W ﻿ / ﻿ 38.89056°N 77.00444°W ﻿ / 38.89056; -77.00444 Composition method Presidential nomination with Senate confirmation Authorised by U.S. Constitution Judge term length Life tenure Number of positions 9, by statute Website supremecourt .gov Chief Justice of the United States Currently John Roberts Since September 29, 2005 The Supreme Court of the United States ( SCOTUS ) is the highest court in the federal judiciary of the United States . It has ultimate appellate jurisdiction over all U.S. federal court cases, and over state court cases that turn on questions of U.S. constitutional or federal law . It also has original jurisdiction over a narrow range of cases, specifically "all Cases affecting Ambassadors, other public Ministers and Consuls, and those in which a State shall be Party." [ 2 ] In 1803, the Court asserted itself the power of judicial review , the ability to invalidate a statute for violating a provision of the Constitution via the landmark case Marbury v. Madison . It is also able to strike down presidential directives for violating either the Constitution or statutory law . [ 3 ] Under Article Three of the United States Constitution , the composition and procedures of the Supreme Court were originally established by the 1st Congress through the Judiciary Act of 1789 . As it has since 1869, the court consists of nine justices – the chief justice of the United States and eight associate justices – who meet at the Supreme Court Building in Washington, D.C. Justices have lifetime tenure , meaning they remain on the court until they die, retire, resign, or are impeached and removed from office. [ 3 ] When a vacancy occurs, the president , with the advice and consent of the Senate , appoints a new justice. Each justice has a single vote in deciding the cases argued before the court. When in the majority, the chief justice dec
perturbation.justification: The original text contains a clause allowing parties to change their domicilium (address for legal notices). The changed text removes this clause. The Perturbation Explanation states that this 'impedes a parties' ability to change its address for legal notices, creating conflicts with civil procedure rules regarding service of legal documents in South Africa.' The Law Explanation states that the changed clause 'creates risk of non-compliance due to impediment of serving legal notice to a correct and updated address, potentially violating fundamental fairness principles in civil procedure.' Therefore, by removing the ability to change domicilium, the changed text creates a potential conflict with legal procedures related to serving legal documents, creating a legal contradiction.
